coredata.dc:title: NBS–rCDs(OH−) chemiluminescence analysis system for the determination of cobalt ions
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Jianqiu
Yu, Yan
Zhang, Zhengwei
Liu, Zhen
Yan, Zhengyu
coredata.dc:description: A novel sensing system has been designed for cobalt ion detection based on a cobalt ion improved chemiluminescence (CL) in N-bromosuccinimide(NBS)-reduced carbon quantum dot (rCD) (OH−) system. The mechanism of this CL analysis system has been preliminarily studied. In this work, it was demonstrated that this facile methodology offers rapid, sensitive and reliable detection for cobalt ions with a detection limit as low as 3.252×10−6 mol/L and a linearity range from 1.004×10−5 to 1.004×10−3 mol/L (r=0.9990), which has promising application prospects.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Reduced carbon quantum dots
Chemiluminescence
Cobalt ions
Determination

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The optimization of the CL system

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Flow injection CL analysis system (a (rCQDs+NaOH), b (water/Co2+) c(NBS), P1, P2: peristaltic pumps, V: injection valve, W: liquid waste, F: flow cell, PMT: photomultiplier tube, HV: negative pressure, AMP: signal amplifier, R: computer).
(a) UV absorption and PL emission spectra of rCDs, Inset: optical images under UV light; (b) TEM images of rCDs; (c) FT-IR spectra: curve i is CQDs, curve ii is rCDs; (d) Size distribution of rCDs.

Effect of reagent concentration (a is NBS: CNaOH =0.5mol/L, CrCDs =2mg/mL, CCo2+
=1×10−4mol/L; b is NaOH: CNBS =0.06mol/L, CrCDs =2mg/ml, CCo2+ =1×10−4 mol/L; c is rCDs: CNBS =0.06mol/L, CNaOH =0.5mol/L, CCo2+ =1×10−4 mol/L).

The linear relationship of cobalt ions detection (a is without cobalt ions, the concentrations of b–f is 1.004×10−5mol/L, 5.020×10−5mol/L, 1.013×10−4mol/L, 5.065×10−4mol/L, 1.004×10−3mol/L, respectively).

The fluorescence spectra of different systems (a: [H2O–rCDs(OH−)–H2O]; b: [H2O–rCDs(OH−)–Co2+]; c: [KMnO4–rCDs(OH−)–H2O]; d: [NBS–rCDs(OH−)–H2O]; e: [NBS–rCDs(OH−)–Co2+] CNBS=0.06mol/L, CrCDs=2g/L, CCo2+ =5×10−4mol/L, CNaOH=0.5mol/L, CKMnO4 =5×10−4mol/L).
The recovery rate of cobalt ions.

head.ce:title: NBS–rCDs(OH−) chemiluminescence analysis system for the determination of cobalt ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of science, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 210009, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel sensing system has been designed for cobalt ion detection based on a cobalt ion improved chemiluminescence (CL) in N-bromosuccinimide(NBS)-reduced carbon quantum dot (rCD) (OH−) system. The mechanism of this CL analysis system has been preliminarily studied. In this work, it was demonstrated that this facile methodology offers rapid, sensitive and reliable detection for cobalt ions with a detection limit as low as 3.252×10−6 mol/L and a linearity range from 1.004×10−5 to 1.004×10−3 mol/L (r=0.9990), which has promising application prospects.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CDs) have several good features, such as their excellent fluorescence stability, low toxicity and biocompatibility [1–3]. They have broad application prospects in the fields of biological imaging, analysis and detection [2,4]. The research on the photoluminescence phenomenon of CDs is widespread and intensive, and research on the chemiluminescence phenomenon has been reported over the past several years. CDs can undergo chemiluminescence when they are oxidized by oxidants such as KMnO4, K3Fe(CN)6, H2O2, KIO4 and Ce(IV) [5–8], but there are no reports on their direct chemiluminescence as a result of reacting with N-bromosuccinimide(NBS) in an aqueous alkaline solution. Research on CDs and the phenomenon of chemiluminescence of NBS helps to explain the photophysical behaviour of carbon quantum dots and their unique properties.

Cobalt is a trace element that plays an important role in the human body [9,10], but it can be harmful to the body if it is present in an excessive quantity [11]. Thus, it is of great significance to perform the detection of cobalt. There are two common types of methods for the determination of cobalt. One is atomic absorption spectrophotometry (AAS) [12], and the other is inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) [13]. Although these methods have high sensitivity and accuracy, the availability of the relevant testing instruments is low. In recent years, researchers have developed several applications using CDs. For example, there are numerous accounts on the use of different CDs for the detection of ions [14–16]. In this paper, we designed an NBS–rCDs(OH−) CL system and detected the concentration of cobalt ion solutions based on the cobalt ion-improved CL intensity of the analysis system. The detection mechanism was also studied.

Starch ((C6H10O5)n, AR, Chinese Medicine Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd), 30% H2O2 (AR, Chinese Medicine Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), acetic acid (HAc, AR, Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd), sodium borohydride (NaBH4, AR, Chinese Medicine Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, AR, Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd.), N-bromosuccinimide (NBS, AR, Chinese Medicine Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.) and cobalt chloride (CoCl2·6H2O, AR, Shanghai Zhenxin Reagent Factory) were purchased. All other reagents were of analytical grade and were used as received. Double-distilled water was used throughout the experiments.

A flow-injection CL analyser (IFFM-D, Xi'an Ruimai Analytical Instrument Co., Ltd.), an RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 particles distribution instrument (Malvern, England), a 98-3 magnetic stirrer (Nanjing Cole Instrument Equipment Co., Ltd.), an RE-52A rotary evaporator (Shanghai Yarong Biochemical Instrument Plant) and a PHS-25pHmetre (Shanghai Precision Scientific Instrument Co., Ltd) were used in this study.

The starch was carbonized and then heated for 12h at 120°C under the action of 30% H2O2 and HAc. The obtained CDs were filtered to remove impurities. The solution was dried and then dissolved in double-distilled water. The pH of the solution was adjusted to 9 with NaOH solution. NaBH4 (0.1mol/L) was used to reduce the CDs at 4°C for 24h. The solution was then dialyzed for 24h. Finally, the solution was dried to obtain the yellow powder, which were the rCDs.

The rCDs were dissolved in NaOH solution (0.5mol/L), and the concentration of the rCD solution was 2g/L. The mixture of rCDs and 0.06mol/L NBS solution produced CL. Cobalt ions improved the intensity of the CL. The mix of each solution is shown in Fig. 1 . The programme for a single sampling pump is shown in Table 1 . The other instrument measuring parameters were as follows: high voltage of 800V, gain of 1, filter cut-off frequency of 50Hz, sampling speed of 10 times/s, and CL signal to peak metre high.

The rCDs give an apparent absorption in the ultraviolet range and have a shoulder peak at 290nm. A strong PL emission peak centred at 460nm can be observed when excited at 350nm, which corresponds to the rCDs emitting bright blue fluorescence under an ultraviolet lamp (Fig. 2a). The TEM images of as-prepared rCDs (Fig. 2b) showed that the rCDs were uniform and monodisperse spherical particles 7nm in size (Fig. 2d).

The FT-IR spectrum was recorded to provide further structural insights into the rCDs (Fig. 2c). The rCDs we obtained contain carbon, oxygen and hydrogen. The IR spectrum in Fig. 2c shows a peak at 3390cm−1 due to an –OH group, which confirmed that the rCDs contain oxygen and hydrogen. Compared with curve i, which shows the presence of a peak at 1590cm−1 attributed to the surface of the CDs containing β-diketone, curve ii presents a weak peak centred at 1625cm−1, and the intensity of peak υ (–OH) increased, suggesting that the carbonyls of the CDs are reduced to alcohol –OH groups after the CDs are reacted with NaBH4. NaBH4 is a selective and mild reducing agent, which only reacts with the carbonyls of aldehydes and ketones. Other groups, such as –COOR, –COOH and –NO2, are free from interference [17]. Moreover, the fluorescence quantum yield was calculated according to the method reported in the literature [18] and was approximately 12%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optimization of the CL system
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, the rCDs were used to construct the [NBS–rCDs(OH−)–Co2+] CL analysis system. The concentrations of NBS, NaOH and rCDs were optimized. As the oxidant in this system, NBS has a significant influence on the CL intensity (see Fig. 3-a). The oxidation is derived from its hydrolysate HBrO. NBS can compensate for the defects of the poor stability of HBrO. Compared with common oxidants, such as Ce(IV) and KMnO4, NBS solution is colourless, so the background interference is low, which helps to improve the sensitivity. At the range of 0.01 to 0.03mol/L, with the increase of the concentration of NBS, the CL intensity also increased. The value of the CL intensity was highest when the concentration was 0.06mol/L. NaOH is an important factor in the system for CL intensity. The mixture of rCD and NBS solutions produced weak CL without NaOH. When NaOH solution was added to the system, the CL intensity was enhanced. Further investigation showed that when the concentration reached 0.5mol/L, the CL intensity was the highest. When the concentration was greater than 0.5mol/L, the intensity decreased (see Fig. 3-b). Finally, 0.5mol/L NaOH was chosen for the dissolution of rCDs. As the luminescent material in this experiment, the rCD concentration was another key factor that affected the sensing system by influencing the CL intensity, sensitivity and linear range of the whole system. The surface of rCDs contains reducing –OH groups, which can be reacted with HBrO. rCDs can release the photon to produce a CL signal in the system because of the high quantum yield. As shown in Fig. 3-c, with the increase in the concentration of rCDs, the CL intensity in this system increased. When the concentration was 2g/L, the intensity was the highest. Therefore, this concentration of rCDs was chosen for determination.

The selectivity of the NBS–rCDs(OH−) sensing system was estimated. According to the above experimental method, different types of the interfering substances were added to detect the CL spectrum in the complex system under a cobalt ion concentration of 1×10−4 mol/L. As shown in Table 2 , within the error range of 5%, various ions in the system do not produce interference, that is, the system has good specificity.

The accuracy of the analysis method was tested by a sample recovery rate experiment. Cobalt ions were not detected in commercial water with this CL system. A 0.03mol/L cobalt ion solution was prepared as the stock solution. Volumes of 1.20mL, 1.00mL and 0.80mL of stock solution were added to 100mL volumetric flasks to obtain high, medium, and low concentrations. Each solution was prepared in triplicate. The control was prepared using the same method without cobalt ions. The CL spectra was detected using the above flow-injection method to test the recovery rate. The results are presented in Table 3 . The recovery rates of the samples were 99% to 106%, and the RSD was 2.1% (n=9). Therefore, the method can be used for the determination of cobalt ions in water.

As mentioned above, the addition of cobalt ions can improve the intensity of CL in the NBS–rCDs(OH−) system. There is a useful relationship between the cobalt ion concentration and the CL intensity that can be adopted for the quantitative analysis of cobalt ions. The linear response range, accuracy and detection limit of the sensing system were measured under the optimum experimental conditions (see Fig. 4 ). There was a good correlation between the ∆I (the incremental CL intensity) and the concentration of cobalt ions in the range from 1.004×10−5 to 1.004×10−3 mol/L via the following equation: ∆I=930860C+39.31 (r=0.9990), where C is the concentration of cobalt ions. The detection limit of cobalt ions (at an S/N of 3) was calculated to be 3.252×10−6 mol/L. At a cobalt ion concentration of 1.013×10−4 mol/L, the RSD was 1.9% (n=11). This value is consistent with the results obtained using another electrochemical method. The results show that the NBS–rCDs(OH−) system with strong CL activity has potential for application in the detection of cobalt ions.

The experiment indicated that both NBS and KMnO4 can react with rCDs to produce CL in the solution. Compared with the fluorescence spectrum of the [H2O–rCDs(OH−)–H2O] system, the fluorescence intensity of the [KMnO4–rCDs(OH−)–H2O] and [NBS–rCDs(OH−)–H2O] system is lower (see Fig. 5 ). The result shows that rCDs in the alkaline solution can interact with oxidizers, such as NBS, in agreement with Mohammad Amjadi [6]. In addition, the fluorescence emission spectrum of [NBS–rCDs(OH−)–H2O] shows that the maximum emission wavelength was blue shifted, whereas the [KMnO4–rCDs(OH−)–H2O] system maximum emission wavelength showed no obvious change due to NBS providing Br− to the surface of the rCD or Br− interference, which shifts the fluorescence emission peak position. Researchers believe that oxidants can form holes in rCDs [6,7,19,20], so BrO− NBS hydrolysed holes in the singly occupied orbital of rCDs and interacted with the active electronic state of the rCDs to produce the CL phenomenon. As the electron acceptor, the singly occupied orbital of rCDs was injected with electrons from OH−, which can combine with holes and release energy in the form of CL.

To explore the sensitization of Co2+, the CL of different components of the mixed solution was obtained. The result shows that the [H2O–rCDs(OH−)–Co2+] system has a weak CL signal due to the Co2+ catalytic rCDs reacting with the dissolved oxygen in the system. This process may involve the production of oxygen active substances, such as
OH and
O2 − [21–24]. These oxygen active substances can interact with each other and produce (1O2)2* or further react with the rCDs. A comparison of the fluorescence spectra of the [H2O–rCDs(OH−)–Co2+] system (Fig. 5-b) and the [H2O–rCDs(OH−)–H2O] system (Fig. 5-a) shows that the presence of cobalt ions decreases the fluorescence intensity. The influence of ascorbic acid and histidine on the [NBS–rCDs(OH−)–Co2+] CL system was also studied. The results indicate that ascorbic acid and histidine have an inhibitory effect on the CL intensity of [NBS–rCDs(OH−)–Co2+] but do not completely eliminate the Co2+ sensitization effect. Therefore, the cobalt ions may also enhance the system's CL signal in other ways. Compared with the [NBS–rCDs(OH−)–H2O] system (Fig. 5-d), the peak position of the fluorescence emission spectrum of the [NBS–rCDs(OH−)–Co2+] system (Fig. 5-e) does not move, but the intensity is decreased.

The literature reported that Co2+ plays a catalytic role and can be oxidized to trivalent cobalt in the CL system [25]. In our work, Co2+ may also be oxidized. By observing the mixed solutions with different components after flowing through the injection CL apparatus, the colour of the [NBS–NaOH–Co2+] system was observed to be blackish green, and sediment was generated. The [H2O–NaOH–Co2+] system was yellow, and a small amount of precipitation appeared after 6 or 7h. Co2+ slowly oxidized to generate Co(OH)3 under the alkaline conditions. The above phenomenon shows that with NBS, Co2+ rapidly converts to Co3+ in the alkaline environment. The colour of the [NBS–rCDs(OH−)–Co2+] system is similar to that of the [NBS–NaOH–Co2+] system but without precipitation. Research has demonstrated the use of Co2+ for the sensitization of the CL intensity of the [H2O2-luminol] system. Cobalt exists in the form of a complex after the reaction [26]. In addition, Shaoqing Li found that the sensitization effect of Co2+ and 1,10-phenanthroline together on the [KIO4-luminol] system is stronger than Co2+ alone [27]. We hypothesized that Co2+ may be combined with rCDs to form a complex in the [NBS–rCDs(OH−)–Co2+] system, so there is no Co(OH)3 generated. The formation of the complex may also help to enhance the CL intensity.

We propose that in an alkaline environment, NBS can be hydrolysed to produce BrO− and then react with rCDs. Cobalt ion catalytic active oxygen, on the other hand, can interact with rCDs to form a complex, which enhances the CL intensity. The specific luminescence process requires further research.

In this paper, we obtained rCDs from the starch that was carbonized by refluxing in a weak acid environment. In the alkaline solution, rCDs mixed with NBS produced a CL signal. Cobalt ions can increase the CL intensity, which was used to design an analysis system for Co2+ detection. Under the optimized conditions, that is, the concentrations of NBS, rCDs and NaOH are 0.06mol/L, 2g/L and 0.5mol/L, respectively, the Co2+ concentration in the range of 1.004×10−5 mol/L to 1.004×10−3 mol/L and the CL intensity had a good linear relationship (r=0.9990). The detection limit was 3.252×10−6 mol/L, and the recovery rate was 99% to 106% (RSD=2.1%, n=9). In addition, the CL mechanism was also studied. BrO− , which is the hydrolysate of NBS, can oxidize the rCDs to produce CL in the form of electronic-hole radiation restructuring. Co2+ combined with the rCDs and catalysed the active oxygen generating, which enhanced the CL intensity of the system.

A novel sensing system has been designed for cobalt ions detection. Chemiluminescence system based on the interaction between metal ions and rCDs can develop a new analytical method for environmental monitoring.
